Notice Of Motion And Motion For Leave To Amend Cross-Complaint **Redacted**
Off Calendar Dec-08-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: DEFENDANT ROBERT BOSCH SEMICONDUCTOR LLC Notice Of Motion And Motion For Leave To Amend Cross-Complaint **Redacted**

No appearance was made by any Party, Counsel, or Interested Person.

The court adopts its tentative ruling. Defendant and cross-complainant Robert Bosch Semiconductor LLC's motion for leave to amend cross-complaint is granted. Bosch shall file the proposed amended cross-complaint attached as Exhibit A to its amended notice of motion forthwith and in no event later than December 12, 2025.

The court exercises great liberality in permitting amendment of the pleadings. (See Nestle v. Santa Monica (1972) 6 Cal.3d 920, 939.) "[I]t is a rare case in which a court will be justified in refusing a party leave to amend his pleadings so that he may properly present his case." (Morgan v. Superior Court (1959) 172 Cal.App.2d 527, 530 [citation simplified].) The court may deny amendment where there is undue delay and prejudice. (Magpali v. Farmers Group, Inc. (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 471, 487.) It may also deny amendment where a proposed claim is futile. (Singh v. Lipworth (2014) 227 Cal.App.4th 813, 828.)

The court finds no undue delay, prejudice, or futility. Bosch presents evidence that the basis for the new claims were learned in October depositions. Although the amendment adds tort claims and fraud, it does not change the tenor or nature of the case in that the pled misrepresentations relate to the formation of a contract that is already at issue. Pakal contends there is prejudice in the delay but offers no specifics. Pakal contends that the individual defendant added by the cross-complaint will suffer prejudice but does not identify discovery he would take that will be foreclosed.

A proposed order which repeats verbatim the above ruling submitted as instructed in the court's tentative ruling.

Judge: Christine Van Aken. Clerk: Robert Woods. Not Reported. (301/CVA)

DEFENDANT ROBERT BOSCH SEMICONDUCTOR LLC MOTION TO STRIKE 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT (TENTATIVE RULING PART 2 OF 2)
Off Calendar Apr-17-2025 Off Calendar

MOTION TO STRIKE Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Feb-06-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT ROBERT BOSCH SEMICONDUCTOR LLC MOTION TO STRIKE Amended COMPLAINT.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Bosch moves to strike those portions of the FAC that demand special damages on the grounds that an entitlement to special damages has not been pleaded with sufficient particularity. "A defendant cannot be presumed to be aware of the special damage resulting from his act, and therefore, in order to prevent a surprise on him, this sort of damage must be specially set forth in the complaint, or the plaintiff will not be permitted to give evidence of it. And an allegation that by reason of the breaches of contract the party has suffered special damages in a named sum, is not enough. The facts as to special damages must be stated with particularity. The amount of such damages must be stated with particularity. The amount of such damages must be given, and the means of occasioning them must be set forth." (Shook v. Pearson (1950) 99 Cal.App.2d 348, 351-352.) Pakal has pleaded damage to its business greater than the loss of its ability to have its semiconductors manufactured alone; it has pleaded that it is a nascent business that has lost reputation and future prospects because of the timing of the alleged breach. But it has not stated the amount of damages and has not stated the "means of occasioning them" beyond broad assertions. Pakal's contrary case authority, MacLeod v. Tribune Publishing Company (1959) 52 Cal.2d 536, 548, acknowledges that a plaintiff may not know the full amount of the special damages it has suffered at the time of pleading but does not excuse pleading those amounts that are known or reasonably estimated.

The motion to strike is granted. Pakal has requested leave to amend to cure the defect; that leave is granted in keeping with California's liberal pleading standards. Pakal may file an amended complaint within 20 days of notice of entry of this order. Bosch's time to respond is extended to 20 days after service of the amended complaint.

Counsel for the prevailing party is to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling with a cover letter reflecting compliance with CRC 3.1312.

PLAINTIFF PAKAL TECHNOLOGIES, INC. NOTICE OF MOTION AND MOTION TO COMPEL: (1) PRODUCTION OF DOCUMENTS, AND (2) FURTHER RESPONSES TO SPECIAL INTERROGATORIES.
Off Calendar Dec-09-2024 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances (by Zoom):
Zack Flood, Esq. 415-279-6343 Lewis Llewllyn LLP for Plaintiff. (In-person)
Matt Alvis, Esq. 415-882-8200 for Defendant.

Argued. Defendant requests to stay ruling pending motions. Mr. Alvis gives the Court an overview of the case. the Court shares its views. Mr. Flood responds. After full argument, the Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

PLAINTIFF PAKAL TECHNOLOGIES, INC.'s MOTION TO COMPEL: (1) PRODUCTION OF DOCUMENTS, AND (2) FURTHER RESPONSES TO SPECIAL INTERROGATORIES.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling.

Plaintiff Pakal Technologies, Inc.'s Motion to Compel (1) Production of Documents, and (2) Further Responses to Special Interrogatories is GRANTED. Plaintiff's RFP Set 2 and SROG No. 2 are reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery admissible evidence on the issues of successor liability and special damages, and good cause appears to compel responses to these discovery requests. Defendant Robert Bosch Semiconductor LLC did not establish that the RFPs are overbroad. Defendant failed to show that any of these requests are unduly burdensome. Defendant's privacy concerns can be addressed by way of protective order. The court does not find that Defendant's response to these discovery requests was substantially justified. The court grants in part Plaintiff's request for sanctions against Defendant and orders Defendant to pay Plaintiff sanctions in the amount of $4,600.

The parties are advised that stacking discovery motions is disfavored. Stacked motions may be ordered off calendar in favor of separate motions focused each on a single discovery vehicle (e.g., Frogs, SRogs, RFPs, RFAs, deposition notice).

Order is signed.
